transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Thank you for calling Sunrise Recovery Center, this is Felicia.

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Hi. My name is Kristin. I overdosed last night. My roommate found me and used naloxone. I'm alive and I need help.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Kristin, I am so glad you called and I am so glad your roommate was there. That was a life-saving moment and you survived it. How are you feeling physically right now?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Weak. Sick. I have a headache. But I'm okay enough to talk.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Good. You're okay. First — have you been seen by a doctor since last night? Did you go to the ER?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: No. My roommate wanted me to but I refused. I was scared.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I understand. I'm going to be honest with you: after an opioid overdose, medical evaluation is important — not just for the overdose itself, but because your body has been through significant stress. I want to get you both medically evaluated and into our detox program today. Not tomorrow — today. Can I ask: are you using heroin, fentanyl, or prescription opioids?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Fentanyl. I've been using for about eight months.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Eight months of fentanyl use and an overdose last night — this is a genuine medical emergency, Kristin, and you're doing exactly the right thing by calling. Here is what I want to do: I'd like to arrange for you to be seen at our medical facility this morning. Our intake team can coordinate transport from where you are. Where are you located?

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Okay. Do you have insurance with you?

transcript.speaker: caller
transcript.text: Yes. CoreFirst Insurance. My card is here. Member ID BP82630580, group GRP-815784.

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: I have that. Kristin, I'm escalating you to our medical intake coordinator right now — they handle same-day admissions for exactly this kind of situation. I need you to stay on the line. Does your roommate know you're calling us?

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Good. Tell them to stay with you until our coordinator calls back — which will be in under ten minutes. Do not drive. Do not be alone. Can you do that?

transcript.speaker: agent
transcript.text: Good. You called at exactly the right moment, Kristin. I'm getting help to you right now.

gold_labels.coaching_notes: Benchmark post-overdose call. Agent correctly identified this as a medical emergency, asked about ER evaluation, named the specific substance (fentanyl), and escalated to same-day medical intake rather than a standard callback. Instructions to stay with roommate and not drive are correct safety protocol. One of the strongest urgency triage demonstrations in the dataset.
